title: Shaping a wet-felted hat without a hat block
subtitle: Using a pot, bowl, or balloon to form a hat crown when a block is unavailable
excerpt: A hat block gives the ideal shape, but a saucepan, a mixing bowl, or an inflated balloon serves as a passable substitute for a first or second hat. This entry covers the substitution options, their limitations, and how to compensate for the most common deformations that occur when shaping on improvised forms.

glossaryTerms.definition: The domed upper part of a hat, which fits over the top of the head. In hat-felting, the crown is shaped first and the brim is stretched or folded after the crown has set.
Stretching and pinning a damp felt piece over a form to impose a final shape as it dries. In hatmaking, blocking determines the head fit and crown profile.

content.text: Shaping a felt hat without a hat block requires choosing a substitute form whose head measurement closely matches the wearer's head circumference. The form is only needed for the
has dried on its form, the hat holds the shape indefinitely.

A 3- or 4-litre saucepan gives a rounded crown close to a round head block. Measure the pot rim circumference before committing; it should be within 2 cm of the wearer's head measurement. A wide-based mixing bowl gives a more bell-shaped crown suitable for a cloche style. A round balloon inflated to the correct circumference is the most adjustable option: inflate, measure, adjust air, and remeasure until the circumference matches. The balloon's softness means the hat must be very firm before blocking or the sides will collapse inward.
Step 1: Full the hat cone to maximum density

Before shaping, the hat must be more fully fulled than when using a proper hat block. The substitute form will not shape the felt as precisely, so the felt needs to be dense enough to hold the pushed-in crown without the sides collapsing. Continue rolling and rubbing until the felt is firm throughout.
Step 2: Fit the damp hat over the form

While still damp, push the hat over the saucepan, mixing bowl, or balloon. Work the felt down over the form by stretching with both hands, alternating sides to keep even tension. The felt should be tight on the form but not tearing.

With the crown sitting on the form, fold or stretch the brim to the desired angle. T-pins pushed through the brim and into a folded towel beneath the form hold the brim flat as it dries. Without T-pins, weight the brim down with small items.

Do not remove the hat from the form until it is completely dry, which takes 12 to 24 hours depending on thickness and ambient temperature. Removing a damp hat will allow the shape to relax. Felt dries faster in a warm room with good air circulation.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to block-free hat shaping.

items.symptom: Crown collapses inward when removed from the form
items.cause: Hat was not dense enough before blocking, or was removed from the form while still damp.
items.fix: Re-dampen the hat, place back on the form, and allow to dry fully. For next time, full the hat for longer before shaping.

items.symptom: Brim is uneven around the circumference
items.cause: Tension was applied unevenly when fitting the hat over the form.
items.fix: While still damp, stretch the shorter side of the brim gently outward to match the longer side. Pin in place and allow to dry.
